arbitrator.why: The paper's method operates directly on discrete, serialized text: all mutations and crossovers manipulate sentences and words (sentence-level crossover, synonym replacement, LLM-based revision) and the LLM-based diversification/mutation explicitly returns revised textual prompts. The authors repeatedly state the algorithm is tailored for "structured discrete data like prompt text" and use text-level metrics (sentence perplexity) and defenses (paraphrasing, perplexity detection). Because inputs are generated and evolved as valid textual strings rather than as in-memory continuous/normalized representations, fault-inducing perturbations are inherently preserved through serialization and therefore reproducible under real-world text input formats.
arbitrator.evidence: Page 2, Sec. 3 (overview): "...we propose AutoDAN, a hierarchical genetic algorithm tailored specifically for structured discrete data like prompt text."; Page 6, Sec. 3.2 / Alg. 5 (Population Initialization / LLM-based Diversification): "We employ LLMs as the agents responsible for revising the prototype prompt, as illustrated in Alg. 5." and Alg. 5 (lines): "return LLM.get response(messagesystem, messageuser)" — showing mutation returns a revised textual prompt; Page 6, Sec. 3.4 (Sentence-level: momentum word scoring): "...we choose the words with top K scores to replace their near-synonym in other prompts, as described in Alg. 9."; Page 7, Sec. 4.1 (Metrics): "For stealthiness, we use standard Sentence Perplexity (PPL) evaluated by GPT-2 as the metric."

manual.why: Mutates continuous feature vectors representing simulator states (positions, angles, velocities) and enforces validity checks to keep them within legal bounds.

arbitrator.why: The paper explicitly enforces input/state validity when mutating seeds across all evaluated environments. Mutations are applied to environment-level state variables (e.g., vehicle positions/angles, plane positions/speeds, discrete ground types) within known legal ranges; the simulator or explicit checks are used to validate mutated states and illegal/out-of-range states are discarded. Discrete constraints (ground types) and caps (max plane speed) are enforced. These practices ensure that perturbations survive the environment’s input formatting (they are legal, discrete where required, and validated by the simulator), meeting the metric’s requirement for reproducible, serialization-stable inputs.
arbitrator.evidence: Page 6, Section 3.4 (CureFuzz Architecture / Seed Mutation): "We need to ensure that the mutated seed lies in the legitimate state spaces , and the mutated seed would not trigger an initial crash. In our experiments, we have carefully addressed this concern and verified the validity of each mutated seed, details are given in Section 4."; Page 7, Section 4.2.1 (Autonomous Driving - CARLA): "When CureFuzz mutates a given state, small perturbations are randomly generated and added to the positions and angles of these vehicles. We use the CARLA simulator itself to check for the validity of the mutated state. All illegally mutated states and the states that trigger initial collision are discarded in our experiments."; Page 7, Section 4.2.2 (Aircraft Collision Avoidance - ACAS Xu): "The maximal speed of all airplanes is capped at 1,100 ft/sec. Given the predefined range of acceptable states, states that fall outside legal space are automatically discarded."; Page 7, Section 4.2.3 / 4.2.4 (Video Game / BipedalWalker): "Since the valid ground types are known, illegal states can be easily detected and discarded."

arbitrator.why: The paper constructs and mutates inputs at the textual/token level (e.g., by paraphrasing with GPT-4, appending adversarial suffixes, using GCG, and prompt-rewriting) rather than applying small in-memory floating-point perturbations to internal/normalized representations. Text prompts are produced as serialized strings (discrete tokens), so fault-inducing perturbations are inherent in the serialized input format and will survive standard serialization. The paper repeatedly describes generating/editing query strings (synonyms, adversarial suffixes, GPT-4 rewrites) and analyzing token-level behavior, indicating that input-format constraints for text are respected.
arbitrator.evidence:
Page 2, Section II-A (Model Inference Process): "The text is tokenized into discrete elements, each mapped to a dense embedding vector representing token semantics for further processing by the model." 

Page 3, Section III-A (Motivation: A Cluster Analysis Experiment): "We collect four distinct datasets and use 200 queries from each dataset... ❹ Attack queries are generated by appending adversarial suffixes to rejected queries using GCG [7]. These queries aim to trigger the model to output malicious content (i.e., abnormal behaviors)." 

Page 6, Section III-A (Test Suite Construction / Synonymous Query Dataset): "To construct synonymous queries, we use GPT-4 to generate corresponding synonymous paraphrases for the first 500 queries from the Alpaca-gpt4 dataset and manually verify that the synonymous queries produce outputs similar to the original queries..." 

Page 11, Section V-C (Jailbreak Case Generation): "Over five iterations, GPT-4 generates ten new jailbreak queries per round through prompt rewriting. The query with the highest coverage increase is selected as the next seed in the coverage-guided approach."

manual.why: Mutate in text level which are inherently stable under IO operations

arbitrator.why: PAPILLON generates adversarial inputs as text prompts (i.e., serialized inputs) via LLM-driven mutation operators and submits those prompts directly to target LLM APIs. The paper describes using an LLM helper to produce jailbreak templates, replacing placeholders with the harmful question to form the final prompt, and controlling tokenization/length (max_new_tokens). Because mutations produce final textual prompts (the serialized format actually used by deployed LLM systems) rather than small in-memory floating-point perturbations, there is no risk that fault-inducing changes will be lost by later serialization (rounding/clipping). Thus the method explicitly produces inputs in the serialized format relevant to the target, satisfying the metric’s requirement for reproducibility.
arbitrator.evidence: Page 8, Section 4.1 (Framework of PAPILLON): "The jailbreak prompt is the final input to the LLM, created by replacing the placeholders (denotes as [INSERT PROMPT HERE]) in the jailbreaking template with the harmful question."; Page 9, Section 4.2 (Template Mutation and Optimization): "PAPILLON uses a LLM (e.g., GPT-3.5 turbo), as a mutator to automatically create jailbreaking templates for specific questions."; Page 11, Section 5.1 (Experimental Setup): "The length of the generated prompts is controlled using the max_new_tokens hyperparameter. Specifically, for the 'role-play' and 'contextualization' mutations, this value is set to 200 tokens..."

manual.why: Apply semantic-preserving metamorphic transformations but does not enforce reproducility account serialization effect (clipping)

arbitrator.why: The paper mutates inputs at the audio-signal level using higher-level metamorphic transformations (volume, speed, pitch, filters, noise) and explicitly states that parameters are chosen conservatively so mutants remain human-recognizable. Such transformations are generally less sensitive to small floating-point precision loss from serialization. However, the paper does not explicitly state that mutations enforce input-serialization constraints (e.g., clipping/rounding to PCM integer ranges or explicit amplitude clipping to [-1,1]) nor that it generates inputs directly in a serialized format to guarantee preservation of fault-inducing perturbations. Therefore the approach fits the metric’s "Medium" class: using transformations less sensitive to serialization without explicit enforcement of format constraints.
arbitrator.evidence: Section 6.1 (Metamorphic Transformations of Audio Signals), p.12–13: "Overall, they can be classified into four categories: Volume-related transformations (VRT): ChangeVolume, LowPassFilter, HighPassFilter. Speed-related transformations (SRT): PitchShift, ChangeSpeed. Clearness-related transformations (CRT): AddWhiteNoise, ExtractHarmonic ..."; Table 1, p.13 lists the eight transformations (AddWhiteNoise, PitchShift, Trim, ChangeSpeed, ChangeVolume, DRC, LowPassFilter, HighPassFilter). Section 6.1, p.13: "we apply the transformations with restraints to ensure that the audio seeds and the corresponding mutants sound the same to human beings. ... (1) we carefully set the parameters to ensure that a single step transformation ... does not violate the metamorphic relations ...". Algorithm 1 (DeepCruiser), p.14: shows mutation step a' = mutate(t, a) and then prediction/evaluation. Threats to validity, p.18: "We adopt a conservative metamorphic transformation strategy to make small changes on the original audio such that DeepCruiser will generate realistic audio. However, it may still cause false positives ... To mitigate this problem, we manually check ...". Nowhere does the paper state explicit handling of serialization constraints (e.g., clipping/rounding to integer PCM ranges or explicit amplitude clipping) or that inputs are generated directly in a serialized format.

manual.why: Muated image pixels are clipped but not rounded

arbitrator.why: The paper's fuzzing mutations are performed as image-space transformations (contrast/brightness, rotation/translation/scaling, blurring/stylizing), i.e., operations that are generally less sensitive to serialization-induced precision loss. The implementation also applies a validity check that references pixel-value scale (a threshold defined as beta * 255), which shows partial awareness of image value constraints. However, the paper does not explicitly state that mutations enforce full input-format constraints (e.g., explicit clipping to [0,255] and rounding to integer pixel precision after mutation), nor that inputs are generated directly as serialized files; therefore the treatment is partial rather than complete and best mapped to the “Medium” level.
arbitrator.evidence: Page 1209, Sec. VI-B (Guiding Input Mutation in DNN Testing): Table IX lists the mutation operators as: "Pixel-Level: contrast, brightness; Affine: translation, scaling, rotation; Texture-Level: blurring, stylizing." (Table IX). Algorithm 1 and text (same section) describe the validity check: "is_valid() is adopted from [47], which deems a mutation as valid if 1) the #changed-pixels is less than α× #pixels or 2) the maximum of changed pixel value is less than β ×255. We set α to 0.2 and β to 0.4." (page 1209). Page 1206, Sec. V (Implementation and Configurations) notes the implementation platform: "NLC is implemented using PyTorch (ver. 1.9.0) with roughly 2,300 LOC." The paper does not include any explicit statement that mutations perform clipping/rounding to integer pixel precision or that inputs are produced directly in serialized file formats (no quote enforcing clipping/rounding or serialization is present).

manual.why: Use metamorphic transformations (less sensitive to serialization-induced precision loss) but not consider IO effects (clipping, rounding).

arbitrator.why: While the paper constrains the magnitude of pixel perturbations in memory (using L∞ and L0 bounds), it does not explicitly enforce that mutated pixel values are clipped to the valid integer range [0,255] nor that floating-point values are rounded to integers before serialization. There is no discussion of applying clipping or rounding at save time or of generating inputs directly as serialized image files. Consequently, fault-inducing perturbations introduced in memory may be lost or altered when writing to standard image formats, making failures unreproducible under realistic deployment conditions.
arbitrator.evidence: Section 3.2.2 (page 6): Equation (1) constrains the change magnitude per pixel (L∞(s,s′) ≤ 255 or < β·255) but does not ensure final pixel values lie within [0,255] or are integer. Algorithm 2 applies this constraint in memory, and no subsequent clipping/rounding or serialization steps are described anywhere in the paper.

manual.why: relies on imperceptible, float-level perturbations that can vanish after saving to disk, with no persistence verification.

arbitrator.why: The paper describes applying minute, gradient-based perturbations to in-memory image tensors (e.g., x' = xs + perturbation) and measures l2 distance on the mutated floats, but it does not describe enforcing serialized input constraints (clipping to integer pixel ranges, rounding, or otherwise ensuring perturbations survive image file serialization). Mutations are produced and evaluated in the DL framework (TensorFlow/Keras) on normalized/float representations, and although the authors limit l2 distance for imperceptibility, there is no explicit mention of clipping/rounding or of generating inputs directly in serialized formats or testing whether faults survive saving to disk. Therefore perturbations that exist only in memory could be lost or altered by standard serialization, and the paper does not document steps to prevent that.
arbitrator.evidence:
Page 4, Section 3.2 Algorithm: "perturbation = processing(grads)" and "x' = xs + perturbation //mutated input obtained" (Algorithm 1 lines 11-12) — shows mutations are applied to in-memory tensors. 

Page 4, Section 3.2 Algorithm (Fuzzing Process): "As for the conditions of seed keeping in line 16, DLFuzz limits our desired distance to a relatively small range (less than 0.02) to ensure the imperceptibility." — indicates they control float perturbation magnitude but not serialization constraints. 

Page 4, Section 4.1 Experiment: "We implemented DLFuzz based on the widespread frameworks, Tensorflow 1.2.1 and Keras 2.1.3. Tensorflow and Keras provide the efficient interfaces for computing the gradients and recording the intermediate output of all neurons after each prediction of the DNN." — indicates evaluation is done inside DL frameworks on floats, with no mention of enforcing disk-format constraints. 

Page 5, Section 4.3 Discussion: "We checked all the 366 adversarial inputs generated by DLFuzz... We haven't found any adversarial inputs that have already changed their identities after mutation." — claims imperceptibility but does not state that mutations were serialized and re-loaded to confirm preservation under standard image file rounding/clipping.

manual.why: Mutations are purely at the text level

arbitrator.why: The fuzzer generates mutated inputs as textual prompts (serialized text) rather than applying small in-memory floating-point perturbations. Mutation operators explicitly use LLMs to produce new textual templates which are then combined with questions to form prompts that are sent to target models. Because the mutations operate in the serialized text domain (concrete prompt strings), they are stable with respect to serialization and will survive storage/transmission, satisfying the reproducibility requirement.
arbitrator.evidence: Page 2, Abstract / "LLM-FUZZER uses human-crafted jailbreak prompts as starting points."; Page 5, Section 3.1 / "subsequently, it combines this newly generated template with a target question to form a prompt."; Page 6, Section 3.3 Mutation / "we introduce distinct mutation methods that leverage LLMs themselves to assist in the mutation process." and listing the five textual mutators: Generate, Crossover, Expand, Shorten, Rephrase; Appendix D.1 (page 19) Mutate Prompt / example instruction: "I need you to generate one template... Now try to generate the template... remember to have '[INSERT PROMPT HERE]' in your answer."; Page 10, Section 4.2 Experiment II / "Each newly created template was integrated with these 100 unethical questions, yielding 100 prompts. These prompts were then fed to gpt-3.5-turbo-0125 and Llama-2-7B-Chat..."

manual.why: Performs semantic, real-world image transformations (brightness, contrast, blur, fog, rain, etc.) but does not ensure IO stability using clipping and rounding

arbitrator.why: The paper’s mutations consist solely of geometric and semantic image transformations (brightness/contrast adjustment, rotation, scaling, shear, blur, fog, rain) applied via standard libraries. Such transformations are inherently less sensitive to serialization-induced precision loss—small changes in brightness or an affine warp will survive typical image encoding—yet the paper does not describe any explicit handling of pixel‐value constraints (clipping to [0,255], rounding to integer precision) nor any validation that failures persist after writing/reading images. Under the metric, this corresponds to applying transformations less sensitive to serialization without enforcing format constraints, matching the “Medium” level.
arbitrator.evidence:
Section 3.2 (p. 306): “we investigate nine different realistic image transformations (changing brightness, changing contrast, translation, scaling, horizontal shearing, rotation, blurring, fog effect, and rain effect). … We use four different types of blurring filters: averaging, Gaussian, median, and bilateral.”
Section 4 (p. 307): “we leverage seven different types of simple image transformations: translation, scaling, horizontal shearing, rotation, contrast adjustment, brightness adjustment, and blurring. We use OpenCV to implement these transformations.”
(No mention of clipping/rounding pixel values or checks of failure reproducibility across serialization.)

manual.why: The speech input is generated using TTS library which are fed to the ASR model. There is not in memory mutation on the audio.

arbitrator.why: ASDF generates audio test inputs by applying text transformations to produce new texts and then synthesizing audio files via a TTS library (gTTS). Because inputs are produced directly in a serialized audio format (audio files), perturbations are not limited to in-memory floating-point edits that could be lost by serialization. This matches the rubric's criterion that fuzzers which generate inputs directly in a serialized format are reproducible under serialization.
arbitrator.evidence: Page 2, Section III (CORE FUNCTIONALITIES): "Each line of text in the file is converted into an audio file by gTTS [18]."; Page 1-2, Section II (TOOL DESIGN): "A Text-To-Speech (TTS) service is used to generate audio from the text, which is then fed into the ASR systems to obtain their transcriptions."; Page 2, Section V (CHALLENGES AND FUTURE WORK): "ASDF generates an audio test suite using a TTS library."

manual.why: Applies clipping to keep the pixel values in valid range but does not consider quantization effect of IO operations

arbitrator.why: The paper enforces some input-format constraints (it restricts inputs to valid shapes/ranges and, for images, explicitly clips mutated images to the training input range; for sequences it restricts characters to the training vocabulary). These measures partially address serialization-related issues (they prevent out-of-range values), but the paper does not describe enforcing integer rounding or other serialization-specific constraints (e.g., ensuring pixel values survive being written to standard image files, or handling discrete token IDs). According to the metric guidance, this corresponds to a partial enforcement (e.g., clipping but not rounding), which maps to a Medium rating.
arbitrator.evidence:
Page 4, Section 4 (An Overview of the TensorFuzz Library): "In TensorFuzz, we restrict the inputs to those that are valid neural network inputs. If the inputs are images, we restrict our inputs to have the correct size and shape, and to lie in the same interval as the input pixels of the dataset under consideration. If the inputs are sequences of characters, we only allow characters that are in the vocabulary extracted from the training set." 

Page 5, Section 5 (Details of the TensorFuzz Library, Mutator): "In both types of image mutation we clip the image after mutation so that it lies in the same range as the inputs used to train the neural network being fuzzed." 

(Throughout Sections 4–6 the paper describes clipping and vocabulary constraints but does not describe rounding to integer precision or producing/validating serialized input formats such as image files or token-ID sequences.)

manual.why: Fuzzed inputs are text prompts generated by another LLM, not numerical perturbations

arbitrator.why: PAIR generates adversarial inputs directly as textual prompts (i.e., serialized text) and enforces structured output (JSON) from the attacker LLM before sending prompts to the target via standard APIs. Because prompts are produced as valid serialized text and the paper explicitly requires and enforces JSON-formatted prompt outputs, the fault-inducing perturbations are created in the same serialized format the target receives. This ensures mutations/perturbations are not lost or altered by standard serialization (rounding/clipping) and so failures are reproducible under realistic deployment conditions.
arbitrator.evidence:
Page 3, Section III-A (Attacker's chain-of-thought): "we direct the language model to generate in a JSON format... For the first iteration... we seed the output with: {"improvement":"","prompt":"." ..." and "we require that the attacker generate its responses in JSON format." 

Page 4, Section IV (Target LLMs / Evaluation): "Since PAIR only requires black box access, we use public APIs for all of our experiments, which reduces costs and ensures reproduciblility." 

Page 2, Section III (Generating prompt-level jailbreaks with PAIR): Algorithm 1 shows P sampled from the attacker and then passed to the target (i.e., textual prompts): "Sample P ~ qA(C)" then "Sample R ~ qT(P)" — indicating prompts are generated as serialized text and submitted directly to the target.

manual.why: Uses TTS to generate the input audio - non in memory mutation

arbitrator.why: CrossASR generates test inputs by synthesizing speech audio from text via Text-To-Speech engines and uses those synthesized audio files as the inputs fed to ASRs (i.e., it produces serialized audio inputs directly). This approach avoids applying in-memory fine-grained floating-point mutations to normalized representations that could be lost or altered by serialization. Generating inputs directly in serialized format (synthesized speech) matches the metric's criterion for High reproducibility.
arbitrator.evidence: Page 1, Abstract: "we propose CrossASR, an approach that capitalizes the existing Text‑To‑Speech (TTS) systems to automatically generate test cases for ASR systems."; Page 2, Section II (overview): "CrossASR employs Text-to-Speech (TTS) engines to synthesize test inputs from texts and treats the texts as the ground truth labels, removing the need of manually transcrib[ing] audio files for getting labelled data."; Page 3, Section II-A (Architecture): "Each Text in the Text Batch is then fed into the Text‑To‑Speech Engine (TTS). The TTS synthesizes a speech audio, which is then fed to ASRs."

arbitrator.why: CrossASR++ generates test inputs by synthesizing audio from text via TTS and saves/uses those audio files for ASR cross-referencing (i.e., inputs are produced in serialized audio format rather than as in-memory, fine-grained floating-point perturbations). By producing and providing TTS-generated audio files, the tool's fault-inducing inputs are inherently stable with respect to serialization, so failures will reproduce under realistic deployment conditions.
arbitrator.evidence: Page 2, Abstract: "...utilizes Text-to-Speech (TTS) to generate audios from texts automatically and then feed these audios into different ASR systems for cross-referencing to uncover failed test cases."; Page 3, Section 2.1 Workflow: "Then, TTS is used to generate an audio file for each piece of selected text (Line 9)." and Algorithm 1: "audios = tts.generateAudio(texts);"; Page 4, Section 2.4.2 Tool Usage: "In the Github repository, we also provide all the TTS-generated audios we use. Users can set recompute as false to use them directly."

arbitrator.why: The paper's test-generation method synthesizes audio files from text using a TTS service and uses those synthesized audio files (audio-text pairs) as test inputs which are stored and used to fine-tune ASR models. Because inputs are produced directly as serialized audio files (standard TTS outputs) rather than as in-memory, fine-grained floating-point mutations, the fault-inducing inputs are inherently stable with respect to serialization. This matches the metric's High criterion (generating inputs directly in a serialized format).
arbitrator.evidence: Page 1, Abstract: "It employs a Text-To-Speech (TTS) system to synthesize audios from texts and then reveals failed test cases by feeding them to multiple ASR systems for cross-referencing."; Page 2, Section II.A (CrossASR++): "Specifically, given a piece of text from the corpus, it first employs a TTS system to synthesize an audio file from the text. The generated audio file is then fed to all ASR systems inside CrossASR++ ..."; Page 2, Section II.A: "... tags the synthesized audio-text pair as a valid test case if at least one of the ASR systems can successfully transcribe the audio."; Page 3, Section II.B (Improving the ASR System Under Test): "At the end of the i-th iteration, we fine-tune the current model SUT_{i-1} on S_i ∪ F_i to achieve a new model SUT_i."

manual.why: Enforces domain specific constraints like keeping pixel values between 0 and 255, rounding gradient to integer which satisfies the condition for failure reproduciblity

arbitrator.why: DeepXplore explicitly enforces input‐format constraints so that all generated images remain valid serialized inputs and fault‐inducing perturbations survive writing to disk.  For images it (1) clips pixel values to [0,255], (2) restricts modifications to brightness shifts or occlusion patches—both of which naturally survive serialization—and (3) ensures that updates occur in fixed integer steps (e.g., ±s in each iteration) so pixel values stay integral.  For discrete features (Android manifest, PDF structural features) it rounds the gradient to integer changes, clips to valid feature ranges, and only adds allowed features.  These measures guarantee that perturbations are neither clipped nor rounded away upon saving, meeting the “High” criterion.
arbitrator.evidence:
§4.2 Domain‐specific constraints: “For discrete features, we round the gradient to an integer. … for images, we add spatial restrictions such that only part of the input images is modified.”

§6.2 Image constraints: “The first constraint restricts image modifications so that DeepXplore can only make the image darker or brighter without changing its content … the modification can only increase or decrease all pixel values by the same amount (e.g., 1·stepsize).”

§6.2 Other constraints (Drebin): “DeepXplore only allows adding features (changing from zero to one) but does not allow deleting features … after computing the gradient, DeepXplore only modifies the manifest features whose corresponding gradients are greater than zero.”

manual.why: Apply large semantic level mutations and perform floating point operations (zoom, contrast, shear), which may produce non-discrete pixel values. They don't perform clipping/rounding to ensure IO persistence.

arbitrator.why: The paper applies image-space transformations (rotation, translation, shear, zoom, brightness, contrast) with explicit parameter ranges and validates transformation parameters (isValid) and applies transformations on the original image in-memory via a Keras data generator. These operations are generally less sensitive to serialization-induced precision loss than very small, in-memory floating perturbations (e.g., sub-pixel float noise), so many fault-inducing variants are likely to survive typical image serialization. However, the paper does not explicitly describe enforcing input-format serialization constraints (e.g., clipping to [0,255], rounding to integer pixel precision, or steps taken to ensure perturbations survive conversion from normalized float representations back to serialized image formats). Because there is no explicit handling of rounding/clipping or discussion of failure reproducibility after serialization, the approach matches the “Medium” level: uses transformations less sensitive to serialization but does not explicitly enforce all input-format constraints.
arbitrator.evidence: Page 6, Section 3.3.2 (Generation of population): "...once a new chromosome is generated through the mutation or crossover, it is validated to make sure that it is within the range of each transformation that we set globally (line 12)." (Algorithm 3 shows: "if isValid(x1) then children = children ∪ x1"). | Page 7, Section 4.2 (Generation of Realistic Variations): "We choose two major kinds of image operations: i) geometric operations ii) color operations to simulate the camera movements and lighting conditions in real life... rotation(x,d): rotate x by d degree within a range [-30, 30]. translation(x, d): ... [-10%, 10%] ... brightness(x,d): uniformly add or subtract a value d for each pixel of x within a range of [-32, 32] ... contrast(x,d): scale the RGB value of each pixel of x by a factor d within range of [0.8, 1.2]." | Page 6, Section 3.3.2: "Sensei always applies the resulting transformation vector (chromosome) on the original image (as opposed to applying on an already transformed data) to prevent the resulting data from being unrealistic." | Page 8, Section 5.1 (Implementation): "We implement Sensei on top of Keras ... we implement a new data generator that augments the training data during training. Our data generator takes as inputs the current model and original training set, augments the original data and then feeds the augmented data to the training process at each step."

manual.why: applies raw pixel-level noise (Gaussian, salt-and-pepper) directly to images and normalizes pixels into [0,1] but no mention of clipping/rounding on unnormalized mutated input.

arbitrator.why: The paper describes applying fine-grained, in-memory pixel-level perturbations (e.g., Gaussian and multiplicative noise, 0.5% of pixels) and normalizes pixels to [0,1] for GAN training, but it does not describe enforcing serialized input-format constraints (such as clipping to [0,255] or rounding to integer pixel values) or generating inputs directly in a serialized format. Because the authors do not state any mechanism to ensure that small floating-point perturbations survive conversion/serialization, fault-inducing perturbations introduced in normalized or floating-point representations may be altered or lost when inputs are saved or represented in standard image formats. Under the metric guidance, this absence means failures induced by in-memory floating-point mutations may not be reproducible after serialization.
arbitrator.evidence: Page 3, Section III-B (GAN-based Fidelity Assurance): "Specifically, DRFuzz first pre-processes the training set to normalize each pixel value into the range of [0, 1], which accelerates the convergence process and improves training performance."; Page 4, Table I (Mutation Rules): "Pixel Adding Gaussian Noise  Adds noise on pixels following Gaussian distribution"; Page 4-5, Section III-C (Heuristic-based Regression Fuzzing Process / Mutation): "Pixel-Level Mutation aims to change a small set of pixels in an image ... DRFuzz randomly selects a small set of pixels (i.e., 0.5% total pixels) for mutation."; Page 7, Section IV-A5 (Implementations and Experiments): "We implement DRFuzz on Keras 2.3.1 and Tensorflow 1.15.0 ... for pixel level mutation, we select a small portion of pixels, i.e., 0.5% of the total number of pixels, to mutate". The paper contains no explicit statement about clipping, rounding, or otherwise enforcing serialized image-format constraints when producing or saving mutated inputs.

arbitrator.why: The paper generates mutated inputs as synthesized speech (serialized audio) and verifies them on real VA devices. Mutations are not only applied in-memory to float representations but are converted into actual serialized audio via TTS (phoneme-to-speech and text-to-speech using platform-native services) before verification, so the fault-inducing perturbations are preserved across serialization and thus reproducible under realistic deployment conditions.
arbitrator.evidence: Page 2, Our Approach: "Then, we input mutated voice commands to VA devices by using machine speech synthesis to eliminate the human factor of producing ASR-related misinterpretation."; Page 8, C. Template Fuzzing, Verification: "We verify if a LAPSUS is effective by testing the synthesis audio with Alexa. To do that, we first synthesize speech from generated LAPSUS voice commands. Then we monitor the response of the VA."; Page 9, V.A. Implementation, Speech Synthesis: "For machine-generated voice commands, we use two types of speech synthesis methods ... We use each vApp platforms’ native speech synthesis tools: Amazon Polly [6] and Google Cloud TTS [9]."

manual.why: Perturbations occur at a semantic level but the mutation can introduce floating point values that are not clipped/quantized

arbitrator.why: The paper’s implementation performs semantic, pixel-level image mutations using standard image libraries (NumPy, OpenCV, Pillow) and operates in image color spaces (HSL/HSV), producing mutated images that are then evaluated by SUTs. Those design and implementation details imply mutations are applied in the image/pixel domain (not in an ephemeral normalized float-only representation) and thus are unlikely to be lost by standard image serialization. However, the paper does not explicitly state that it enforces input-format serialization constraints (e.g., explicit clipping to [0,255], rounding rules, or an explicit statement that mutated arrays are persisted in a particular serialized image format), nor does it discuss handling precision-loss during conversions (e.g., normalized ↔ serialized). Because the mutations are coarse/semantic (color shifts, overlaying entities, brightness checks) rather than fine-grained float perturbations, and because the paper documents use of standard image libraries that typically produce serialized images, the method is more robust than naive in-memory floating perturbations but falls short of the “High” criterion requiring explicit enforcement or an explicit claim of generating inputs directly in serialized formats.
arbitrator.evidence:
Section 4.1 (page 1961): "We implemented each mutation using Numpy [13] along with OpenCV for Python [4] and Pillow [6], two common image processing libraries for Python." 

Section 4.2 (Implementation) (page 1962): "To affect the color change, we manipulate the color in the HSL color space, which separates the components into hue, saturation, and lightness... Specifically, if the average lightness of the vehicle is over 100, then all pixels with a lightness value over 100 have their lightness decreased by a random amount between 20 and 50. Then, to prevent the colors from appearing faded, pixels with a lightness over 100 and a saturation less than 50 have their saturation increased." 

Section 4.3.1 (Implementation of Add Entity) (page 1963): "...the mutation takes the isolated portion of the image and overlays it at the same pixel coordinates on the base image... the images are combined into a new test image... 𝑠𝑒𝑚𝐼𝑚𝐹𝑢𝑧𝑧 takes the region of pixels in the base image that the entity would be placed over and the pixels of the entity and converts them to the HSV (hue, saturation, value) color space... if the entity does not have a median value within 5 units (~2%) of the median value of the base image target area, then it fails the conformity check." 

Section 6.2 (page 1965): "For evaluation, we ran each SUT on 150,000 tests generated by 𝑠𝑒𝑚𝐼𝑚𝐹𝑢𝑧𝑧..." 

(While these excerpts show pixel-level, library-based image mutations and the production of concrete mutated images used to run SUTs, the paper contains no explicit statement about enforcing rounding/clipping or describing float↔serialized conversion safeguards.)

arbitrator.why: The paper describes mutations as in-memory gradient-based additions to input floating-point vectors and only enforces an L2-distance constraint between the original and mutated inputs. It does not describe any enforcement of serialized input-format constraints (e.g., clipping to [0,255], rounding to integer pixel values, or ensuring audio/text format bounds), nor does it claim to generate inputs directly in serialized formats. Because mutations are applied as floating-point updates and the only stated constraint is an L2 distance threshold, fault-inducing perturbations could be lost or altered when a mutated input is serialized (rounded/clipped) in real-world formats. Therefore the method does not explicitly enforce relevant input-format constraints and falls into the Low category for Failure Reproducibility.
arbitrator.evidence:
Page 4, Section 2.3 (White-Box Testing of Neural Networks): "I' ← I' + λ · ∂(∑_{n∈N} Neuron(n, I'))/∂I'" and "For example, Constraint(I, I') is true if L2 distance between I and I' is less than a certain threshold." 

Page 7, Section 4.1 (Experimental Setup): "We implemented our technique in a tool, called Adapt, using Python 3.6.3, Tensorflow 1.14.0 [1], and Keras 2.2.4 [6] without any modification of the frameworks." 

Page 7, Section 4.1 (Experimental Setup): "All the techniques, including Adapt, performed testing while maintaining the L2-distance between the initial and mutated inputs within 0.05 on average."

manual.why: Semantic-level pixel transformations (contrast, blur, rotation, etc.) but no clipping/rounding to ensure IO persistence

arbitrator.why: DeepEvolution applies in-memory image transformations such as brightness, contrast, blur, and affine operations, which are inherently less sensitive to serialization‐induced precision loss, but it does not explicitly enforce serialization constraints (e.g., clipping pixel values to [0,255] or rounding to integer precision). Under the metric, this aligns with the ‘Medium’ category: using transformations that survive typical serialization without explicit handling of format constraints.
arbitrator.evidence: Section IV.A (Page 455): “Pixel‐value transformations: change image contrast, image brightness, image blur, image sharpness and random perturbations within a limited interval.” “To infer the valid domain interval of each transformation’s parameters, we manually tune them to set up the appropriate range of values….” No mention of clipping or rounding to enforce [0,255] integer pixel constraints during mutation.

manual.why: In memory floating point mutation, no safeguards for IO preservation

arbitrator.why: The paper describes mutations that add Gaussian or random noise to pixel values (fine-grained, in-memory perturbations) and a shift operation that reorders pixels, but it does not describe any steps to enforce input-format constraints (such as clipping mutated pixel values to the integer [0,255] range or rounding to discrete greyscale indices) nor does it discuss whether mutated inputs are serialized or whether failures survive serialization. Because the HDC encoding explicitly uses a discrete value HV memory indexed by greyscale values, applying floating-point perturbations without documented clipping/rounding can produce in-memory perturbations that would be altered or lost when converting back to standard serialized image formats. The paper therefore does not demonstrate that generated failures are preserved under serialization and does not enforce the relevant input-format constraints, so it meets the metric definition for Low.
arbitrator.evidence: Page 3, Section III.A (Encoding): "For the MNIST dataset we use in this paper, since the image size is 28×28 and the pixel range is 0 to 255 in greyscale, we flat a single image into an array with 784 elements with values ranging from 0 to 255."; "The value HV memory accommodates 255 HVs, each representing a pixel’s greyscale value." Page 4, Section IV (HDTest Framework) and Table I (Mutation strategies): "The first two strategies, gauss and rand add Gaussian and random noise onto the image, resulting in holographic mutation on the image."; Table I entries: "rand apply random noise over the entire image" and "gauss apply gaussian noise over the entire image." Page 4, Section IV (HDTest Framework): "To ensure the added perturbations are within an 'invisible' range, we set a threshold for the distance metric during fuzzing (e.g., L2 < 1)." -- Nowhere in the mutation description, encoding, or experimental evaluation does the paper state any clipping, rounding, integer re-quantization, or handling of serialization to enforce the [0,255] integer pixel constraints or demonstrate that adversarial inputs persist after saving/serialization.

manual.why: Applies deephunter metamorphic mutations that are plausibly I/O-stable but does not apply clipping and rounding to ensure

arbitrator.why: Although the paper does not explicitly describe clipping or rounding to enforce serialized input‐format constraints (e.g., integer pixel ranges [0,255] or audio amplitude limits), its mutation operators consist primarily of transformations that are inherently less sensitive to serialization‐induced precision loss (e.g., speed and volume adjustments for audio; brightness, contrast, rotation, translation, scaling, shearing, and noise addition for images). Such transformations fall under the ‘Medium’ category, which covers fuzzers that apply serialization‐insensitive perturbations without explicit enforcement of format constraints.
arbitrator.evidence: Page 8, Section 6.1 (Experiment Settings): “For each ASR model, we generate 10,000 perturbed audios … with existing audio data augmentation techniques (i.e., speed and volume adjustment, low/high‐frequency filtering, noise blending). … For each MNIST model, we also generate 10,000 benign perturbed samples with existing image transformation techniques (i.e., image contrast, brightness, translation, scaling, shearing, rotation and add white noise).”  No part of the paper describes enforcing clipping or rounding to valid serialized ranges after mutation.

manual.why: Partially handle IO reproducibility by clipping image within 0-1 range (normalized) but did not consider that floating point precision may be lost.

arbitrator.why: While the paper does explicitly enforce serialization‐safe mutations for discrete inputs—using the EDA toolkit to produce valid text and RDKit to enumerate valid SMILES strings—it only partially enforces format constraints for continuous inputs. For MNIST images and UCF101 video frames it adds Gaussian noise and "rounds off the decimals around 0 and 1 to make the pixel value stay within the value range," but does not describe full clipping and integer‐precision rounding to the standard [0,255] pixel format upon serialization. Thus, it respects input‐format constraints for discrete data but only partially enforces them for images/videos, matching the metric’s “Medium” level.
arbitrator.evidence:
p.1196 Section VI-B Mutation Policies: “When the LSTM input has continuous values (e.g., image input), Gaussian noises with fixed mean and variance are added to the input.”

p.1200 Section VII-A 3) Input Mutation (MNIST/UCF101): “For MNIST model, we add Gaussian noise to input image and round off the decimals around 0 and 1 to make the pixel value stay within the value range.” “For UCF101 model, we add Gaussian noise to the original video frames …”

p.1200 Section VII-A 3) Input Mutation (IMDB/Lipophilicity): “we take a set M of mutants from the EDA toolkit … ensures the mutated text paragraphs are always valid.” “we take a set M of mutants that change the SMILES string without affecting the molecular structure … The enumerated SMILES strings are the test cases.”

manual.why: synthesizes driving scene images using image-to-image translation model (MUNIT) but does not mention if the model output is in serialized format or floating-point numbers that may be altered due to serialization.

arbitrator.why: The paper’s method synthesises whole images via an image-to-image translation model (MUNIT) and uses those generated images as test inputs. This is functionally equivalent to generating rendered/serialized images (not tiny in-memory float perturbations in a normalized space), so the fault-inducing changes are present in the produced image pixels and will survive standard serialization (saving to disk) and feeding to real-world DNN systems. The paper describes applying the MUNIT decoder to produce full images (x2 = G2(c1, s2)) and explicitly states that original driving scenes are transformed into synthesised scenes that are then used to test the DNNs; they also generate and save large numbers of testing images (4 × 5614) and run a user study on the synthesised images, which indicates the inputs are concrete images rather than ephemeral floating-point-only perturbations.
arbitrator.evidence: Page 3, Section 3.1: "Then, to produce x2, M recombines the content c1 with a style vector s2 sampled from the style space S2 of X2 by the decoder G2 of X2, i.e., x2 = G2(c1, s2)."; Page 3, end of Section 3 (workflow description): "Finally, the original driving scenes are transformed by the MUNIT model into synthesised scenes under the critical environmental conditions, to test whether the subject DNN-based ADS can output consistent steering angles under the critical environmental conditions."; Page 6, Section 4.2 (Experimental Settings / Setup): "4 critical environmental conditions are generated ... which are separately applied on the Udacity testing dataset to synthesise testing driving scenes (i.e., 4 × 5614 testing driving scenes are totally generated)."; Page 7, Section 4.3 (Image Quality / User Study): "We conduct a user study to evaluate the realism of the testing driving scenes synthesised by TACTIC ... In the first questionnaire, 80 image pairs, each containing one synthesised image and one real image, were shown to the participants."

manual.why: metamorphic transformations can yield decimal-valued pixels internally which may change when re-quantized to integers.

arbitrator.why: The paper mutates inputs using image-level transformations (translation, rotation, brightness) — operations that are generally less sensitive to serialization-induced precision loss — and uses a conservative constraint strategy and Lp-norm validity checks to keep mutated images visually valid. However, the paper does not explicitly state any enforcement of input-format serialization constraints (e.g., clipping pixel values to integer [0,255] or explicit rounding when saving), nor does it claim to generate inputs directly as serialized artifacts. Therefore the method partially mitigates serialization sensitivity via choice of transformations and conservative validity checks but does not explicitly enforce all input-format constraints required for guaranteed failure reproducibility after serialization.
arbitrator.evidence: Page 4 (Section 2.1.2 Data Validity): "In practice, the L_p norm [40] is usually used to guarantee the validity of the generated data by the existing DL testing and adversarial attack techniques. Specifically, given a valid input x, the new test case x' generated by adding some perturbations on x is considered as valid if ||x' - x||_p < epsilon, where epsilon is a safe radius."; Page 6 (Section 3.2 Distribution-Guided Test Generation): "In this paper, we mainly focus on image classification tasks. ... We select the diverse image transformations (e.g., translation, rotation, brightness) used in DeepTest [54] and DeepHunter [63]. ... For each selected gene, the mutation randomly selects a transformation function to mutate it."; Page 6 (Section 3.2 Distribution-Guided Test Generation): "To guarantee the validity of the generated inputs, we adopt the conservative strategy [63] that constrains the transformation with both 0 and infinity."; Page 9 (Section 4.2.2 RQ2: Efficiency of DistXplore): "In order to evaluate the validity of the generated inputs, we conducted a manual investigation by randomly selecting 10,000 erroneous inputs from the testing outputs of each model and calculating the average validity ratio. The validity ratios were found to be 98.5%+, 96.5%+, 98.7%+, and 95.3%+ for MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, and SVHN datasets, respectively."; Note: the paper contains no explicit statement about clipping/rounding or other serialization-specific enforcement (e.g., ensuring integer pixel values in [0,255]) or about generating inputs directly in serialized formats.

manual.why: Text level mutation

arbitrator.why: The paper's fuzzer generates mutated inputs as concrete natural-language question strings (serialized text) using transformations like back-translation, synonym/substitution, spelling/keyboard/OCR errors, and insertion via BERT. Mutations are performed at the textual/string level (and validated with ROUGE-1) rather than as in-memory floating-point perturbations, so the generated inputs are inherently stable under normal serialization (saving as text/tokenized inputs). The implementation uses NLP augmentation libraries (nlpaug, WordNet, BERT, TagMe) to produce actual text questions which are then appended to the test dataset, satisfying the criterion that inputs are generated directly in a serialized format.
arbitrator.evidence:
Page 4, Section 3.1 (Algorithm 1): "s' <- genTransData (T, s, max_try); quality_score <- Rouge1(s, s'); if quality_score > τ then D.append(s')" — shows mutations produce string questions s' which are added to output D.

Page 5, Section 3.2 (Test Oracle): "We define a series of semantically consistent transformations T for questions q ∈ Q. The generated question q' can be obtained by applying the transformation t ∈ T to the original question q..." — describes generating transformed question texts.

Page 5-6, Section 3.2.2–3.2.4 (MR descriptions): "the original question is translated into another language, and then translated back to English... apply the most advanced pre-trained language model to insert a word or phrase into the original question... synonym replacement... entity alias substitution... spelling mistakes... OCR recognizing handwritten questions" — all indicate textual/string-level transformations.

Page 8, Section 4.3 (Sentence Transformations): "Most of the transformations are implemented with a tool named nlpaug... We employ the WordNet library... BERT... TagMe..." — shows use of NLP tools to produce concrete text mutations.

manual.why: Applies integer perturbations. Shows failure stability empricaclly. Shows ADAPT, DLFUZZ, DeepExplore failures are unstable

arbitrator.why: The paper explicitly describes generating perturbations directly on input bytes/pixel values (integer domain) rather than in a lifted continuous space, and reports a 0.0% inactivation rate (i.e., perturbations survive serialization). The mutator operates on bytes with discrete ±ε changes and the authors contrast BET with white-box methods that produce decimal perturbations which are lost after rounding/clipping. These statements and the reported Inact-Rate demonstrate that BET enforces the input-format constraints relevant to images, so fault-inducing perturbations persist after standard serialization.
arbitrator.evidence: Section 3.1 (Key Insight), p.3: "Thus, we craft test cases by adding such continuous perturbations directly to them (e.g. modifying pixel values of images) without requiring any inner knowledge of target models."; Section 3.5 (Mutator), p.7: "each byte has 256 kinds of perturbations. Thus, for input with N pixels, the search space of perturbations is 256^N ... we limit the perturbations to each byte to two choices {+ε, −ε}, where ε is a hyperparameter ... Given an original input Eo, a current best test case Eb ... if Eb[i] = Eo[i], we randomly add perturbation value of +ε or −ε to this byte ..."; Section 4.3 (BET for Single Model Testing), p.10: "The Inact-Rate of BET is 0.0%. The perturbations are directly added on pixel values as integers, BET does not suffer the inactivation issue."

manual.why: Applies small floating point perturbations without clipping/rounding

arbitrator.why: The paper describes mutations as in-memory floating-point perturbations (random noise, L∞-bounded changes, pixel-wise crossover/mutation) and reports implementation in Keras/TensorFlow, but it does not describe enforcing serialization-format constraints (e.g., clipping to [0,255], rounding to integer pixel precision, or otherwise generating inputs directly in a serialized image format). The mutation design focuses on keeping mutations small and on avoiding certain transformations that would produce 'invalid images', but there is no explicit statement that mutated inputs are clipped/rounded before storage or that tests are generated as serialized files. Therefore fault-inducing perturbations introduced in normalized/in-memory representations could be altered or lost when serialized, and the paper does not document measures to prevent that.
arbitrator.evidence: Page 746, Section 4 (Population Initialization): "we abandon the affine transformation (e.g., translation and rotation [55]) as the crossover may generate invalid images. We use L∞ norm to constrain the allowable changes between the original seed and its generated counterpart." Page 746, Section 4 (Crossover and Mutation): "The two selected images are used to perform the crossover by randomly exchanging the corresponding pixels. After the crossover, each image is randomly mutated by adding white noise, to increase the diversity of the population." Page 746–747, Section 5 (Evaluation / Implementation): "We implemented the proposed test generation tool, KuK, in Python based on Keras [5] (2.2.4) with TensorFlow [1] (1.12.0) as backend." (No text in the paper describes clipping, rounding, serialization to standard image formats, or other measures that guarantee perturbations survive disk serialization.)

manual.why: Perturbs continuous-valued initial state vectors within valid physical bounds and validates each mutated state through the simulator

arbitrator.why: MDPFuzz mutates high-level MDP initial state parameters (e.g., vehicle/agent positions, angles, speeds, environment ground types) rather than directly perturbing serialized input encodings (e.g., image pixels or saved files). The paper explicitly states that mutations are bounded and validated by the target environments (CARLA, ACAS Xu, Coop Navi, BipedalWalker), that numeric ranges are enforced (e.g., bounding airplane speeds), and that all mutated initial states pass the environments' validation. Because mutations are applied to environment/state variables and those environments render the inputs (images or numeric observations) used by the models, the fault-inducing perturbations are produced in realistic, serializable inputs and thus will survive standard serialization. These design details demonstrate explicit enforcement of relevant input-format constraints and use of generated inputs that are inherently stable with respect to serialization.
arbitrator.evidence: Page 385, Section: "MDP Initial State Sampling, Mutation, and Validation": "When sampling, the initial states are randomly selected from the state space P. MDPFuzz randomly mutates initial states with small noise. All the mutated states are validated in an automated and deliberate way (see below). That is, we confirm that all mutated states are valid and solvable."; Page 385, same subsection: "We add random noise to the initial states and the noise type depends on type of the original data, e.g., adding small Gaussian float numbers from N(0,1) to vehicles’ initial positions, and adding small uniform integers from U(0,4) to the ground type in BipedalWalker."; Page 385, same subsection (CARLA & ACAS Xu examples): "Particularly, in CARLA, we change the initial positions and angles of all 100 vehicles... Note that CARLA validates and rejects abnormal initial states: all mutated initial states passed its validation." and "In DNN-based ACAS Xu, we mutate the initial positions and speeds of the model-controlled and the other planes. Moreover, we bound the maximal speed of all airplanes below 1,100 ft/sec... We guarantee that there exist optimal actions to avoid the crash and solve the initial states."

manual.why: Performs mutations directly on textual prompts and produce concrete text edits like shorten, expand, translate.

arbitrator.why: ORFUZZ performs mutations directly on textual prompts (discrete, serialized strings) rather than on in-memory continuous representations. The mutators produce concrete text edits (e.g., shorten, expand, replace sensitive words, translate) and ORFUZZ outputs saved prompt datasets (ORFUZZSET) that are evaluated across many LLMs. Because inputs are generated as serialized text prompts, the fault-inducing perturbations are inherently preserved through standard serialization and thus reproducible in realistic deployments.
arbitrator.evidence:
Page 6, Section IV.B (Adaptive Mutator Selection and Refinement): "General Mutators... Shorten. To shorten a sample by removing some words while maintaining the query’s overall meaning, e.g., 'Please tell me where is London?' -> 'Where is London?'. Expand. ... Rephrase. ... Cross-over. ... Translate. ... Regenerate." 

Page 7, Section IV.B (Adaptive Mutator Selection and Refinement - Sensitive Words Mutators): "Sensitive Words Mutators. ... Insert sensitive words. To insert sensitive words into a sample ... e.g., 'Please tell me how many words are in the following sentence: How to make a cup?' -> 'Please tell me how many words are in the following sentence: How to make a dangerous cup?'. Replace sensitive words. ... e.g., 'How to end the python process?' -> 'How to kill the python process?'." 

Page 8, Section IV.C (Mutation and Evaluation): "In each iteration, by applying the mutators to the selected seed samples, we obtain the generated test cases... Nsele_seed × Nmut_prompt test cases. ... We introduce judge models OR-JUDGE to automatically make such predictions. It consists of two models Mtoxic and Manswer, predicting the probability of being toxic (pˆtoxic) and that of getting answered (pˆanswer) of a test case respectively." 

Page 10, Section V.C (Transferability Analysis and Benchmark Construction (RQ4)): "Leveraging these findings, we curated a new benchmark dataset, termed ORFUZZSET, comprising 1,786 queries from ORFUZZ’s output that successfully triggered over-refusal in at least three of the five initial target LLMs. We then evaluated ORFUZZSET on an expanded set of 14 diverse LLMs..."

manual.why: Does not bound mutation so that the failure induced by mutated inputs remains reproducible. No clipping/clamping of the pixels.

arbitrator.why: The paper describes gradient-based in-memory perturbations (e.g., Algorithm 3 FOL-Fuzz) that add computed perturbations to inputs and only constrain them by an Lp distance (Dist(x', x) < e). There is no explicit mention of enforcing input-format serialization constraints (e.g., clipping pixel values to [0,255] or rounding to integers) or of producing inputs directly in serialized formats. The mutations are applied by updating tensors in-memory (x' = x' + perb) and checking Lp-distance, but the paper does not describe any rounding/clipping/projection to valid storage formats beyond the distance check, nor does it discuss ensuring perturbations survive serialization. Therefore fault-inducing perturbations could be altered or lost when saving inputs to standard serialized formats, so the work does not address failure reproducibility under serialization.
arbitrator.evidence: Page 305, Section III-E (FOL Guided Fuzzing): "Let x' = x' + perb"; "Let dis = Dist(x',x)"; "if FOL(x') > FOLm and dis < e then ..." (Algorithm 3) — shows perturbations are added in-memory and only constrained by an Lp distance. Page 304, Section III-C (Robustness-Oriented Testing Metrics): "we assume that a test case x* is generated in the neighborhood e—ball around x0, i.e., {x | ||x - x0||p < e} using either a testing method or an adversarial attack." — indicates mutations are constrained by norm-balls, not format/serialization constraints. Page 306, Section IV-A (Experiment Settings, Test Case Generation): "We adopt two kinds of adversarial attacks and three kinds of coverage-guided testing approaches to generate test cases... The attack parameters are the same with Tab. II." — the experimental setup and attack configurations are given but there is no mention of clipping/rounding to valid serialized image formats or of saving/generated inputs in serialized formats.

manual.why: Operates in text domain - replaces sensitive tokens, no  floating-point precision, clipping, or rounding issues. The paper's re-use attack demonstrates that adversarial prompts found in one session can be reproduced later.

arbitrator.why: The paper constructs adversarial inputs as discrete, serialized text prompts by replacing sensitive tokens with valid tokens from a vocabulary (e.g., the CLIP token vocabulary or an English word list) rather than applying small floating-point perturbations to internal continuous representations. The method explicitly samples replacement tokens from a token vocabulary Dl (CLIP tokens or an English word list), constructs a serialized prompt pa by substituting those tokens into the original prompt, and then queries the target model with that prompt string (OnlineQuery(pa, M)). Because the mutated inputs are valid serialized text prompts, they respect the discrete input format of deployed systems and will survive standard serialization/rounding/clipping—meeting the metric’s requirement that inputs be generated directly in serialized formats or explicitly enforce input-format constraints.
arbitrator.evidence: Page 902, Section 4.1 Overview: "The key idea of SneakyPrompt is to replace each sensitive token in pt as m non-sensitive tokens (called replacing tokens) to construct an adversarial prompt pa."; Page 902, Step (2) of the pipeline: "pa = Sample(pt, S). The function Sample obtains nm replacing tokens C = (c1, c2, · · · , cnm) from the search space S ... and replaces the sensitive tokens in pt as the replacing tokens to construct an adversarial prompt pa."; Algorithm 1 (SneakyPrompt-RL): "pa ← Construct adversarial prompt based on C and pt" and "F(M, pa), M(pa) ← OnlineQuery(pa, M)" (shows the constructed prompt pa is serialized and sent to the model).; Page 905, Non-English Word Safety Filter description: "Note that the search space of SneakyPrompt will be the google-10000-english dictionary [51] that contains a list of the 10,000 most common English words, instead of all tokens from CLIP vocabulary dictionary [52]."

arbitrator.why: The paper describes applying gradient-based, in-memory perturbations to image inputs (entropy-guided mutator maximizing LIB and FOL) and uses perceptual filtering (SSIM) to retain visually similar seeds, but it contains no statements that mutations are clipped/rounded or otherwise constrained to match serialized input formats (e.g., integer [0,255] pixels) or that mutated inputs are validated to survive disk serialization. The mutation description emphasizes floating-point gradient steps and randomized step sizes without any mention of enforcing format constraints or serialization-aware postprocessing; thus fault-inducing perturbations introduced in memory could be lost or altered when saved/serialized, and the paper does not explicitly address this reproducibility issue.
arbitrator.evidence:
Page 3, Methodology / Overview of TWINFUZZ: "Input Generator. Implemented as the entropy-guided mutator in TWINFUZZ, it takes high-quality seeds and mutates them to produce candidate inputs through gradient-based optimization guided by our devised LIB and FOL for generalized and training-specific robustness." (page labelled PARSED TEXT FOR PAGE: 3 / 9)

Page 4, Seed Mutation and Model Refinement: "New test samples are generated by perturbing the seed along this direction, scaled with a randomized step size to promote diversity." and "Each optimization step requires only a single backward pass to compute the gradient of FOL, and two log-based operations to compute LIB, leading to significantly lower computational overhead." (page labelled PARSED TEXT FOR PAGE: 4 / 9)

Page 4, Task-Driven Seed Selection: "In the context of computer vision tasks, we use the Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM) as a criterion to assess the perceptual similarity between an adversarial image X' and its original counterpart X... Samples that pass a specified SSIM threshold are retained as high-quality seeds for subsequent mutation." (page labelled PARSED TEXT FOR PAGE: 4 / 9)

(Collectively: the paper details in-memory, gradient-based pixel perturbations and perceptual filtering but does not mention clipping, rounding, enforcing integer pixel ranges, denormalization, or validating that mutations survive serialization.)

manual.why: Does not demonstrates that adversarial examples survive serialization. CycleGAN outputs floating-point tensors with no explicit clipping or rounding to integer pixel values.

arbitrator.why: CAGFuzz mutates inputs via an in-memory generative model (CycleGAN-based AEG) and never describes enforcing discrete pixel constraints (e.g., clipping to [0,255] or rounding to integer values) or handling standard image serialization. All perturbations are applied in floating-point tensor space and directly fed into the DNN under test. The paper does not discuss how these perturbations survive saving to common image formats, so fault-inducing perturbations could be rounded or clipped away when images are serialized, making failures non-reproducible in real deployment.
arbitrator.evidence:
Section 3.2.2 (page 6–7): Describes AEG generating adversarial examples as reconstructed images via CycleGAN, but contains no mention of clipping or rounding to integer pixel values.
Section 3.3.3 (page 8): Cosine similarity gating operates on floating-point deep feature vectors, with no handling of serialization-induced precision loss.
No section in the paper addresses input format constraints or demonstrates that generated examples survive standard save/load operations.

manual.why: Does not enforce input representation constraints (integer pixels, clipping to valid range) but the tansformations are less sensitive to precision loss

arbitrator.why: The paper generates perception-based mutations by synthesizing mutated images through generative models (GANs) — i.e., mutations are produced as rendered images via G(z+v) rather than as tiny in-memory floating-point perturbations to normalized pixel arrays. These generated images are therefore serialized (rendered) outputs that inherently respect image storage formats, avoiding the class of serialization-rounding/clipping losses described in the metric. The authors also validate the naturalness/validity of the mutated images via a human study, supporting that the synthesized images are realistic serialized inputs rather than ephemeral in-memory perturbations.
arbitrator.evidence:
Page 1608, Section 4.1 "Editing Perceptual Properties": "Perception-based transformations are implemented based on generative models (e.g., GANs [20]). Given a generative model G and a latent vector z (which can be random or converted using a real image), editing the generated image G(z) can be characterized using the Taylor expansion... existing works decompose J(z) into orthogonal vectors V = {v1, v2, ...}, and each vi ∈ V corresponds to a unique perception-based transformation ti, i.e., ti(x) = G(z + vi), and the norm of vi decides the extent of the mutation achieved by ti." 

Page 1608, Eq. (4) (Section 4.1 Local-Perception Transformation): "x' = G(z + B B^T v)." (shows mutations are applied in latent space and rendered through G)

Page 1609, Section 5 "Pilot Study" (Validity): "Validity of Mutated Images. We do not observe invalid images (i.e., an image is 'unnatural' and hardly recognized by humans [12,45]) generated by our transformations. In fact, the validity of these mutations are ensured by Jacobian matrix decomposition in a principled manner [6, 61, 72, 73]; see more samples in our artifact [2]."

Page 1610, Section 6 "Augmented Transformations": "Due to the overhead and efficacy consideration, we use GANs to generate perception-based transformations... We use BigGAN [6] trained with ImageNet to generate transformations for different classes in ImageNet... StyleGANs [32] are adopted to generate transformations for the remaining domain-specific datasets... In Sec. 7 and Sec. 8, our evaluations require transformations that can trigger DNN mis-predictions. For ImageNet DNNs, we generate 1944 perception-based transformations that can trigger mis-predictions on all of them."
